title: Motor Won't STOP — The Runaway
workOrder: WORK ORDER #214 — Operator reports: "I hit Stop and the motor keeps running." Line has been E-stopped at the panel breaker. There are TWO Stop pushbuttons in series (local + remote). Do NOT assume — find which component is lying to you.
symptom: The motor keeps running after the Stop button is pressed. It's a DIFFERENT fault each time you open this ticket. Some faults keep the coil alive; one kills the coil but the motor shaft still turns. Meter the circuit — test points separate the control rung from the motor power branch.

explains.STOP:welded_closed: You pressed the LOCAL Stop and nothing happened — RUN light stayed on, motor kept spinning. Then you pressed the REMOTE Stop (STOP2) and the motor died instantly. TP-2 never lost voltage during your local Stop press; a good NC contact would drop TP-2 to 0V when pressed. The local Stop button contact is physically welded shut — it CAN'T open. Classic: the operator hammers a worn button a thousand times and the silver contacts fuse. Replace the pushbutton. Before you do, verify TP-3 also held voltage during the local press so you know the fault is between TP-1 and TP-2, not further upstream.
explains.STOP2:welded_closed: Local Stop (STOP) killed the motor fine; REMOTE Stop (STOP2) did nothing. TP-3 stayed at 24V during the STOP2 press — it should have gone to 0V. The remote Stop button contact is welded. Happens all the time on remote E-stops at the end of a 50-ft cable: vibration loosens the enclosure, water gets in, contacts corrode then weld on the next overcurrent event. TP-2 to TP-3 is your locating bracket — 24V across STOP2 while 'pressed' is the fingerprint. Replace the remote pushbutton.
explains.MC1:welded_closed: Here's the tricky one. You pressed Stop — the RUN light went OFF, TP-4 (coil input) dropped to 0V, the coil is DEAD. But the motor shaft is still turning. TP-M reads 24V with the coil de-energized. The CR1 main power contact (MC1) is physically welded closed — the contactor armature may have dropped but the silver contact tip fused to the stationary tip. The CONTROL rung is telling you the truth; the POWER contact is lying. Kill the breaker, pull the contactor, and inspect the tips — you'll see the weld. Never assume a dead coil means a dead load; always check TP-M.
explains.START:welded_closed: The first thing you noticed should have been odd: the motor was ALREADY running before you touched anything — RUN light on, STP light off, right from the jump. The START button contact is welded made, so it permanently feeds the coil through n3→n4 without needing the seal-in. When you press Stop the motor dies — BUT the instant Stop releases, the START contact brings it right back. TP-3 reads 24V even with no buttons pressed; on a healthy circuit TP-3 is 0V at rest (only live when START is held or the latch is in). That 24V-at-rest on TP-3 is your tell. Replace the Start pushbutton.
